title: Alzheimer's Disease: Early Signs and What They Mean
summary: Alzheimer's is the most common cause of dementia, but its early signs are often subtle enough to explain away. Here's what tends to show up first.
author: HarborCare Circle Editorial
seoTitle: Alzheimer's Disease: Early Signs and What They Mean | HarborCare Circle
metaDescription: Alzheimer's is the most common cause of dementia, but its early signs are often subtle enough to explain away. Here's what tends to show up first.
whatYouNeedToKnow: Memory lapses for recent events (not old ones) are usually the earliest sign.
Getting lost in familiar places is a bigger flag than misplacing keys.
A diagnosis takes time and rules out other causes first.

paragraphs: Early Alzheimer's usually shows up as trouble with recent memory — forgetting a conversation from yesterday, asking the same question again shortly after it was answered, or losing track of dates. This is different from normal aging, where someone might forget a name but remember it later, or take longer to learn something new without losing the ability entirely.

A second early flag is difficulty with familiar tasks or navigation — getting confused on a regular drive, or struggling to follow a recipe used for years. These changes tend to be gradual, which is part of why families often notice them only in hindsight.

nextSteps: Keep a simple log of specific incidents, with dates.
Schedule a cognitive evaluation with their primary doctor, who can refer to a specialist if needed.
Take the Care Readiness Assessment to help organize what you're seeing.
disclaimer: This describes general early patterns, not a diagnostic checklist. Memory changes have many causes, several of them treatable — a proper evaluation matters more than self-diagnosis.

sources.name: 10 Early Signs and Symptoms of Alzheimer's & Dementia
